quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Energy levels"?

options.text: F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
Discrete allowed energies for electrons: E_n = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen; increasingly closer together for higher n
A quantum of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hf; momentum p = h/λ = hf/c; no rest mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Energy levels" is defined as: Discrete allowed energies for electrons: E_n = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen; increasingly closer together for higher n. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Photon emission"?

options.text: Electron drops from n_high to n_low: emitted photon has E_photon = |E_high - E_low| = hf; energy is conserved
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision; not due to instrument limitations but inherent to nature
1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Photon emission" is defined as: Electron drops from n_high to n_low: emitted photon has E_photon = |E_high - E_low| = hf; energy is conserved. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Photon absorption"?

options.text: Electron absorbs a photon with exactly E = E_upper - E_lower and jumps to a higher level; must match exactly
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei
Light behaves as waves (interference, diffraction) and particles (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering); complementary descriptions
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: "Photon absorption" is defined as: Electron absorbs a photon with exactly E = E_upper - E_lower and jumps to a higher level; must match exactly. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ionization energy"?

options.text: Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)
Weak attraction to B fields; atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles that partially align with the field; effect disappears when field is removed
Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ionization energy" is defined as: Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Spectral series"?

options.text: r = mv/(qB); radius of circular path depends on mass, speed, and charge-to-field ratio; the basis for particle accelerators
Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy
Lyman (to n=1, UV), Balmer (to n=2, visible), Paschen (to n=3, IR); named groups of transitions
β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Spectral series" is defined as: Lyman (to n=1, UV), Balmer (to n=2, visible), Paschen (to n=3, IR); named groups of transitions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Discrete allowed energies for electrons: E_n = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen; increasingly closer together for higher n"?

options.text: Buoyant force (Archimedes)
Energy levels
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Average power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Energy levels".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electron drops from n_high to n_low: emitted photon has E_photon = |E_high - E_low| = hf; energy is conserved"?

options.text: Nuclear fusion
Photon emission
Second Law of Thermodynamics
Compound microscope

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Photon emission".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electron absorbs a photon with exactly E = E_upper - E_lower and jumps to a higher level; must match exactly"?

options.text: Photon absorption
Reflecting telescope
Photon
De Broglie wavelength

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Photon absorption".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)"?

options.text: Ionization energy
Magnification
Convex mirror
Chain reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ionization energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Lyman (to n=1, UV), Balmer (to n=2, visible), Paschen (to n=3, IR); named groups of transitions"?

options.text: Index of refraction
Beta-minus decay
Spectral series
Charge conservation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Spectral series".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Energy Levels in Atoms is accurate?

options.text: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
Energy Levels in Atoms explains the discrete energy structure of atoms.
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy Levels in Atoms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
Fission is the basis of nuclear reactors; fusion powers the Sun.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy Levels in Atoms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: Photon emission occurs when an electron transitions from a higher to a lower energy level: ΔE = E_final - E_initial = hf.
Like mirrors, the thin lens equation 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f applies (with sign conventions).
De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.
Fission: a heavy nucleus (e.g., ²³⁵U) splits into lighter nuclei + neutrons + energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy Levels in Atoms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.
Absorption is the reverse: a photon with exactly the right energy promotes an electron to a higher level.
Fission: a heavy nucleus (e.g., ²³⁵U) splits into lighter nuclei + neutrons + energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy Levels in Atoms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.
Each element has a unique spectrum.
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.
The energy level diagram shows allowed states; arrows between levels represent possible transitions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy Levels in Atoms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions
Ultrasound imaging: high-frequency sound waves reflect off internal structures; the time delay and intensity of reflected waves produce medical images of organs and fetuses

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: Lasers: stimulated emission occurs when photons trigger excited electrons to release identical photons—producing coherent, monochromatic, intense light
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Energy Levels in Atoms applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Night vision devices use the photoelectric effect: photons from dim scenes eject electrons from a photocathode, which are then amplified to produce a visible image
Fluorescent lighting: UV photons from mercury vapor excite phosphor coating electrons, which emit visible light as they return to lower energy levels
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Energy Levels in Atoms?

options.text: Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Quantum computing chips use superconducting circuits (transmon qubits) cooled to 15 millikelvin—colder than outer space—to maintain quantum coherence for computation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Energy Levels in Atoms in practice?

options.text: Lasers: stimulated emission occurs when photons trigger excited electrons to release identical photons—producing coherent, monochromatic, intense light
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential
Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy Levels in Atoms.
